artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

I’m finally sitting down with some tea after taking Daisy out around the block. It’s been a rough couple of weeks here at 4821 NE 72nd Street, and I just needed to talk to you a bit.

The neighbors in Unit 206 have gotten really out of hand. The worst was the night of Feb 24–25. Their music and shouting went on past 2 a.m., and around 1:30 a.m. I ended up having a full-blown panic attack in my bedroom in 304. I’ve been managing the generalized anxiety pretty well with weekly telehealth sessions through Sound Mental Health since August 2024, but that night everything just tipped over. I actually messaged my therapist, Dr. Amanda Lewis, through the patient portal at 2:05 a.m., and we added a 45‑minute emergency video session for 7:30 p.m. that evening to help me calm down and adjust my medication schedule.

Work has felt the impact too. On Feb 12 I overslept my 7:00 a.m. remote start time after another late night of noise from 206 and missed two morning meetings. My manager, Elena Rodriguez at Northwest Integrated Health Partners, gave me an informal written warning and noted it in Workday, which really scared me because she said another incident could affect my 2026 performance bonus. I even ended up paying my $327 Chase Freedom bill late that day because I was so out of it.

I don’t want you to worry too much—I’m still doing my walks with Daisy, cooking simple dinners, and trying to get to bed by 10:30 when the building is quiet. I just hate feeling on edge every night, wondering if the bass is going to start up again.

Love,
Melissa

Noise + impact log – started 2025-12-15
Location: Unit 304, 4821 NE 72nd St, Seattle, WA 98115

Purpose: Keep track of patterns so I don’t gaslight myself into thinking I’m “too sensitive.”

2025-12-15 – First time I really noticed the late-night bass from Unit 206 (Brandon Lee / Jessica Nguyen). Music past midnight, some yelling.

2026-01-05, 1:05 a.m. – Female guest in hallway outside 206 shouting about her probation officer. Wrote down exact phrase at the time because it startled me.

2026-01-19, ~10:45 p.m.–1:00 a.m. – Loud music again. I ended up drinking a full 375 ml half-bottle of Columbia Crest cabernet alone in the living room just to try to relax. Overslept my 7:00 a.m. video check‑in with our clinical team the next morning. Journaled later that I’m worried I’m sliding back toward the unhealthy drinking patterns I had in 2019, before my primary care doc at UW Neighborhood Ravenna Clinic helped me cut back.

2026-02-10, 11:30 p.m.–12:50 a.m. – Party noises, heavy bass. At 12:47 a.m. I recorded a 2‑minute audio clip from my bedroom; can clearly hear someone in 206 talking about buying $60 of weed from a guy in Capitol Hill.

2026-02-12 – Noise again the night before; overslept 7:00 a.m. start, missed two meetings, got informal warning from Elena in Workday, paid $327 Chase Freedom late.

2026-02-24/25, 10:30 p.m.–~2:00 a.m. – Worst so far. Panic attack around 1:30 a.m.; Daisy shaking. Messaged Dr. Lewis at 2:05 a.m.; scheduled emergency telehealth that evening.

Reminder: Breathe. You’re not imagining this.

Hey Laura,

Catching up from my little nest here in 304 with Daisy snoring next to the desk. I owe you a long ramble, so buckle up.

The neighbor situation in Unit 206 (Brandon and Jessica) has gone from annoying to genuinely messing with my life. Remember how I said they were loud sometimes? On Jan 19 they went past 1 a.m. again. I sat in my living room, listening to the bass come up through the floor, and ended up polishing off a 375 ml half‑bottle of Columbia Crest cab just to get sleepy. Then I slept through my 7:00 a.m. video check‑in with the clinical team. It felt way too much like 2019, before my doc at UW Neighborhood Ravenna Clinic helped me cut back on drinking.

Then on Feb 12, same pattern: late‑night noise, I barely slept, and I completely missed my 7:00 a.m. remote start and two morning meetings. Elena had to put an informal warning in Workday and even mentioned my 2026 performance bonus might be on the line if it happens again. To top it off, I spaced on my $327 Chase Freedom payment and made it late that afternoon because my brain was mush.

The worst was the night of Feb 24–25. Their party was so loud I had a panic attack around 1:30 a.m. in my bedroom. I’ve been doing weekly telehealth with Dr. Amanda Lewis at Sound Mental Health since August 2024, and I actually messaged her through the portal at 2:05 a.m. We added a 45‑minute emergency session at 7:30 p.m. that night to talk through it and tweak my meds.

Outside of that, life is mostly the same: walking Daisy around NE 72nd, trying new sheet‑pan dinners, and fantasizing about a quiet little house somewhere with no shared walls.

Love you,
M

Daisy – behavior / anxiety notes

Context: Living in Unit 304 above 206 at 4821 NE 72nd. Working with Mark Jensen at Green Lake Canine Behavior since November 2025 on her separation anxiety after the scare on 2025-09-03 (guy tried to force his way in behind me at the front door). Mark’s been great about giving us exercises.

2025-11–2026-01 – Weekly sessions with Mark near Green Lake. Focus on crate comfort, short departures, desensitizing hallway noises. Overall improvement until the neighbor parties ramped up.

2026-01-05 – Loud hallway voices near 1 a.m.; Daisy pacing and whining in the bedroom. Took ~30 minutes of “settle” work to calm her.

2026-02-10 – Noise from 206 again. During the 12:47 a.m. audio clip I recorded for myself, Daisy was curled up but ears pinned back; occasional low growls when someone in 206 talked about buying $60 of weed in Capitol Hill.

2026-02-24 night – Had her sleep in bed with me in the Unit 304 bedroom because I expected it to be loud. Party in 206 escalated after 11 p.m. Constant thumping and shouting. Around 1 a.m. she started shaking and panting, full body tremors. At 1:20 a.m. I gave her 0.5 mg of the trazodone from Ballard Animal Hospital (per vet instructions for extreme anxiety). She settled about 30–40 min later but still startled at sudden bangs.

Plan: Ask Mark at next Green Lake Canine session how to handle unpredictable building noise. Also ask Ballard vet if there’s anything else we can do so we’re not relying on meds so often.

Event: Emergency telehealth with Dr. Amanda Lewis – Sound Mental Health
Date: Wednesday, 2026-02-25
Time: 7:30–8:15 p.m. (45-minute session)
Location: Telehealth from living room, Unit 304, 4821 NE 72nd St
Attendees: Me, Dr. Lewis

Notes:
– Schedule added after messaging Dr. Lewis through Sound Mental Health portal at 2:05 a.m. on 2/25, right after panic attack triggered by noise from Unit 206.
– Goals for session:
  • Process panic attack around 1:30 a.m. in bedroom (heart racing, shaking, felt like I couldn’t breathe).
  • Talk about ongoing late‑night noise pattern from Brandon Lee and Jessica Nguyen in 206 (music, shouting, heavy bass).
  • Review coping tools for nights when I can’t control the environment (grounding, paced breathing, maybe getting out of bed with Daisy and reading on the couch).
  • Discuss whether to adjust current anxiety medication schedule to better cover evenings, given that I usually start work at 7:00 a.m. and can’t afford to lose so much sleep.
  • Ask if there are any red flags she wants me to watch for re: sliding back into 2019‑style coping (e.g., drinking a half‑bottle of wine on bad nights like 2026-01-19).

Pre‑session prep: Finish work by 6:30 p.m., quick walk with Daisy around the block, set up laptop and headphones in living room, white noise on in bedroom in case 206 starts up again.

Hi Elena,

I wanted to follow up on our conversation from 2/12 and give you some context, as well as reassure you that I’m taking things seriously.

The missed 7:00 a.m. start and two morning meetings on 2026-02-12 were directly tied to ongoing late‑night noise issues in my building here in Seattle. I live at 4821 NE 72nd Street, Unit 304, and the tenants in Unit 206 (directly below me) have been having frequent late gatherings. The night before the incident, their music and shouting went well past 1:00 a.m. I got very little sleep and ended up oversleeping my alarm. I also realized later that day that I’d made my $327 payment on my Chase Freedom card late because I was so foggy.

As we discussed, I completely understand the need for the informal written warning in Workday and the note about potential impact on my 2026 performance bonus if this becomes a pattern. I value my role at Northwest Integrated Health Partners and don’t want my reliability to be in question.

Since then I’ve put a few things in place: multiple alarms on my phone, a backup alarm clock across the room, and a stricter bedtime routine on nights before early meetings. I’m also continuing weekly telehealth therapy through Sound Mental Health with my therapist, Dr. Amanda Lewis (we even added a 45‑minute emergency session on 2/25 after a particularly bad night) to work on anxiety and sleep strategies.

I appreciate your understanding and will keep you posted if the housing situation changes. In the meantime, I’m committed to making sure my work performance and attendance stay consistent.

Best,
Melissa

Journal – 2026-02-20

Another gray Seattle Friday, another night wondering what 206 will do.

I’ve been keeping that detailed noise log in my Notes app since 2025-12-15, and reading back through it tonight is both validating and depressing. There’s the 12:38 a.m. entry where I wrote “heard Brandon yell ‘I don’t care if they call the cops’” and the 1:05 a.m. 2026-01-05 note about the woman in the hallway shouting about her probation officer. And then the 2‑minute audio clip from my bedroom on 2026-02-10 at 12:47 a.m. where you can clearly hear someone in 206 talking about buying $60 of weed from a guy in Capitol Hill.

The part that scares me is how much this has started to bleed into everything else. On 2026-01-19 I drank a 375 ml half‑bottle of Columbia Crest cab alone in the living room after their music went past 1:00 a.m., and then I overslept my 7:00 a.m. video check‑in. It felt like 2019 again, before my UW Neighborhood Ravenna Clinic doctor helped me pull back from using wine as a sleep aid.

I keep thinking about how much I like this apartment otherwise—my little standing desk by the window, Daisy’s bed tucked next to it, our walks around NE 72nd. I don’t want to uproot everything because of one unit, but my body tenses every time I hear footsteps downstairs after 10 p.m.

For now: light dinner, chamomile tea, no wine, and an early night if the building cooperates.

Notes to self:

- Complaint Filed By: Melissa J. Carter
- Time Of Incident: 11:45 PM
- Dated This: 2.0
